---
pagetitle: "Chapter 1: Introduction"
engine: knitr
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    toc: false
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "STA258 | Chapter 1"
    pdf-separate-fragments: false
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    webr:
      packages:
        - tibble
        - ggplot2
    live:
      show-solutions: true
---

# Chapter 1: Introduction {.title-slide}

<style>
.reveal {
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h1,
.reveal h2,
.reveal h3 {
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h2 {
  border-bottom: 3px solid #00695c;
  padding-bottom: 8px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
}

.title-slide {
  text-align: center;
}

.defbox {
  background: #c8e6f3;
  padding: 18px;
  border-left: 7px solid #00695c;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.9em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.examplebox {
  background: #eeeeee;
  padding: 18px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.9em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.thinkbox {
  background: #fff8e1;
  padding: 18px;
  border-left: 7px solid #f9a825;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.9em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.big {
  font-size: 1.25em;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #00695c;
}

.footer-note {
  font-size: 0.65em;
  color: #666666;
}

.center {
  text-align: center;
}

.small-text {
  font-size: 0.75em;
}
</style>

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

STA258: Statistics with Applied Probability  
University of Toronto Mississauga

![](cat.png){width="300"}

::: notes
Use this as the opening slide. The cat image keeps the course style familiar and consistent.
:::

---

## Roadmap {#roadmap}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Core Concepts

- [1.1 Foundations](#foundations)
- [Population vs Sample](#population-and-sample)
- [Unit, Observation, Variable](#unit-observation-variable)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Main Distinctions

- [Parameter vs Statistic](#parameter-vs-statistic)
- [Types of Data](#types-of-data)
- [Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics](#descriptive-vs-inferential-statistics)
:::
:::::

::: notes
This roadmap works as a navigation slide — bullet points link to later slides.
:::

---

## 1.1 Foundations {#foundations}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Definition 1.1: Statistics</strong><br>
Statistics is the science of collecting, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting data.
</div>

<br>

Statistics helps researchers use data to support conclusions about a larger group.

. . .

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Main idea:</strong> Statistics helps us move from data to evidence, and from evidence to decisions.
</div>

::: notes
Emphasize that statistics is not just formulas — it is about learning from data when there is uncertainty.
:::

---

## Population and Sample {#population-and-sample}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Population</strong><br>
The entire group of individuals, items, or measurements we want to study.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Sample</strong><br>
A smaller subset selected from the population.
</div>
:::
:::::

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Key idea:</strong> We usually study the sample because studying the entire population is too difficult, expensive, or impossible.
</div>

---

## Example: Population and Sample

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Population:</strong> All students at the University of Toronto.
<br><br>
<strong>Sample:</strong> 50 randomly selected students from the University of Toronto.
</div>

<br>

<span class="big">Main idea:</span>  
We study the sample to learn about the population.

::: notes
Make sure students understand that the sample is not the final target — the population is.
:::

---

## Unit, Observation, Variable {#unit-observation-variable}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Unit</strong><br>
The smallest entity from which data are collected.
</div>

<br>

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Observation</strong><br>
The collection of measured values for one unit.
</div>

<br>

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Variable</strong><br>
A characteristic that can be measured or recorded.
</div>

---

## Example: Unit, Observation, Variable

<div class="examplebox">
If we record a student's age, height, and weight:
<br><br>
<ul>
<li>The student is the <strong>unit</strong></li>
<li>The full row of recorded values is the <strong>observation</strong></li>
<li>Age, height, and weight are <strong>variables</strong></li>
</ul>
</div>

. . .

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Quick check:</strong><br>
If a dataset has 100 students and 4 measured characteristics per student, how many observations are there?
</div>

---

## Population Unit vs Sample Unit

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Population unit</strong><br>
One member of the full population.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
If the population is all UTM students, one UTM student is a population unit.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Sample unit</strong><br>
One member of the selected sample.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
If the sample is 80 selected UTM students, one selected student is a sample unit.
</div>
:::
:::::

---

## Dataset Language

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Unit | Who or what data are collected from | One student |
| Observation | Full row of data for one unit | Age, height, weight for one student |
| Variable | Characteristic being recorded | Age |

<br>

<div class="footer-note">
This table is useful when students first learn how to read datasets.
</div>

---

## Parameter vs Statistic {#parameter-vs-statistic}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Parameter</strong><br>
A numerical quantity that describes a population.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Statistic</strong><br>
A numerical quantity that describes a sample.
</div>
:::
:::::

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Memory trick:</strong><br>
Parameter → Population &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; Statistic → Sample
</div>

---

## Parameter vs Statistic: Examples

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Parameters (population-level):</strong>
<ul>
<li>Population mean, $\mu$</li>
<li>Population standard deviation, $\sigma$</li>
<li>Population proportion, $p$</li>
</ul>

<br>

<strong>Statistics (sample-level):</strong>
<ul>
<li>Sample mean, $\bar{x}$</li>
<li>Sample standard deviation, $s$</li>
<li>Sample proportion, $\hat{p}$</li>
</ul>
</div>

::: notes
Students often mix up $p$ and $\hat{p}$. Stress that $p$ is population-level and $\hat{p}$ is sample-level.
:::

---

## Parameter vs Statistic: Summary Table

| Population Quantity | Sample Quantity | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $\mu$ | $\bar{x}$ | Mean |
| $\sigma$ | $s$ | Standard deviation |
| $p$ | $\hat{p}$ | Proportion |

. . .

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
We use sample statistics like $\bar{x}$ or $\hat{p}$ to estimate population parameters like $\mu$ or $p$.
</div>

---

## Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics {#descriptive-vs-inferential-statistics}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Descriptive statistics</strong><br>
Methods used to analyze, summarize, and represent data.
</div>

<br>

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Inferential statistics</strong><br>
Using sample information to make conclusions about a larger population.
</div>

---

## Descriptive vs Inferential: Examples

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Descriptive

<div class="examplebox">
The average mark in a class of 80 students is 72%.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Inferential

<div class="examplebox">
Based on 80 students, we estimate the average mark for all STA258 students.
</div>
:::
:::::

---

## 1.2 Types of Data {#types-of-data}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Quantitative data</strong><br>
Data that can be measured numerically.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Examples: height, weight, age, temperature
</div>

. . .

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Question:</strong><br>
Does every number automatically mean the variable is quantitative?
</div>

---

## Qualitative Data

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Qualitative data</strong><br>
Data that cannot be measured numerically — instead, it falls into categories.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Examples: favourite colour, eye colour, ice-cream flavour
</div>

---

## Quantitative vs Qualitative

| Data | Type | Reason |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Height | Quantitative | Measured numerically |
| Number of courses | Quantitative | Counted numerically |
| Favourite colour | Qualitative | Falls into a category |
| Student number | Qualitative | The number is only an identifier |

::: notes
The student number example is useful — it shows that numbers are not always quantitative.
:::

---

## Discrete and Continuous Data

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Discrete data</strong><br>
Quantitative data that are countable.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Examples: number of siblings, number of defects
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>Continuous data</strong><br>
Quantitative data measured on a scale.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Examples: height, time, temperature
</div>
:::
:::::

---

## Quick Check: Types of Data {#quick-check-types}

Classify each variable:

1. Number of courses a student is taking
2. Student's major
3. Time spent studying per week
4. Whether a student lives on campus

. . .

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Answers:</strong>
<ol>
<li>Quantitative, discrete</li>
<li>Qualitative</li>
<li>Quantitative, continuous</li>
<li>Qualitative</li>
</ol>
</div>

---

## 1.3 Introduction to Inferential Statistics {#introduction-to-inferential-statistics}

<div class="center">
<span class="big">Sample → Statistic → Estimate population parameter</span>
</div>

<br>

Statistical inference uses sample data to estimate population values and measure uncertainty.

$$
\text{Sample} \rightarrow \text{Statistic} \rightarrow \text{Parameter}
$$

---

## Big Picture

<div class="examplebox">
We usually cannot measure the whole population.
<br><br>
So we collect a sample, calculate a statistic, and use it to estimate the unknown parameter.
</div>

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Important:</strong><br>
Because samples vary, inference must always include a measure of uncertainty.
</div>

---

## Main Inference Tools

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Estimation</strong><br>
Using sample data to guess the value of a population parameter.
</div>

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Confidence intervals</strong><br>
Giving a plausible range of values for a population parameter.
</div>

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Hypothesis testing</strong><br>
Checking whether sample data support a specific claim.
</div>

::: notes
Pause before revealing the answers. This is a good check for whether students understand sample vs population and statistic vs parameter.
:::

---

## Chapter 1 Summary {#chapter-1-summary}

- Statistics is the science of learning from data
- A sample is used to study a population
- Parameters describe populations; statistics describe samples
- Data can be quantitative or qualitative
- Quantitative data can be discrete or continuous
- Inference uses samples to draw population-level conclusions

---

## End of Chapter 1

<div class="center">
<br>
<span class="big">End of Chapter 1</span>
<br><br>
<a href="test.html">← Back to Home</a>
</div>

::: notes
Update the link if the landing page file name changes.
:::

---

## Optional Interactive R Slide {.scrollable}

Students can edit the code and run it directly inside the slide.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
#| caption: Try changing the commute times

commute_time <- c(20, 35, 40, 15, 60, 25, 30)
mean(commute_time)
```

---

## Optional Practice Exercise {.scrollable}

Change the code so it calculates the sample mean of the vector.

```{webr}
#| exercise: mean_exercise

commute_time <- c(20, 35, 40, 15, 60, 25, 30)
______(commute_time)
```

::: {.solution exercise="mean_exercise"}
::: {.callout-tip title="Solution"}
Use:

```r
mean(commute_time)
```
:::
:::
